title: List Accounts with Login Shells
hook: Login shells are the first account inventory to review.
problem: You need to separate human or interactive accounts from service accounts in a passwd-style file.
starting_state: A fixture-local passwd file contains root, service accounts, deploy accounts, and emergency accounts.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads a fixture-local passwd stub and prints accounts with shell-like login programs.
when_to_use: Use during server handoff, user access reviews, and first-response checks before changing accounts.
when_not_to_use: Do not disable accounts from this list alone; confirm ownership, automation, and operational purpose first.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Usernames and login shells for accounts that can start an interactive shell.

shorts.title: Who can get a shell?
shorts.voiceover: Start an access review by listing accounts with shell-like login programs, then verify whether each one still has a reason to exist.
shorts.caption: Login shells deserve review.

linkedin.question: Do you inventory login-capable accounts before changing server access?
linkedin.cta: Separate shell users from service accounts first.

youtube.description: List accounts with login shells from a fixture-local passwd file.

title: Find Password-Enabled Accounts
hook: A shell account with an unlocked password hash deserves extra attention.
problem: You need to identify accounts whose shadow field is not locked with ! or *.
starting_state: A fixture-local shadow file uses locked markers for most accounts and demo placeholder hashes for two accounts.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads the fixture-local shadow stub and prints accounts whose password field is not locked.
when_to_use: Use during access audits when you need to distinguish locked accounts from accounts that may accept password authentication.
when_not_to_use: Do not infer SSH password login policy from shadow alone; also check sshd configuration and PAM policy on real systems.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Account names with non-locked password fields.

shorts.title: Find unlocked passwords.
shorts.voiceover: In a shadow file, locked accounts usually start with an exclamation mark or star. Filter for accounts that do not.
shorts.caption: Unlocked password fields need review.

linkedin.question: Do you check locked versus password-enabled accounts during access reviews?
linkedin.cta: Pair shadow review with SSH policy before changing anything.

youtube.description: Find accounts whose shadow password field is not locked.

experiments.variant_b: Which accounts can still use passwords?

title: Review sudo Grants
hook: Privilege paths should be visible before you remove or approve access.
problem: You need a compact view of sudo group membership and sudoers rules from fixture-local stubs.
command: awk -F: '$1=="sudo" {print "sudo group: " $4}' fixtures/user-access-audit/etc/group; grep -RhnE '^[^#].*ALL=' fixtures/user-access-audit/etc/sudoers fixtures/user-access-audit/etc/sudoers.d
starting_state: Fixture-local group and sudoers files include group-based admin access and command-limited deploy rules.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads fixture-local group and sudoers stubs and prints privilege grants.
when_to_use: Use when auditing who can elevate privileges or when preparing to remove stale access.
when_not_to_use: Do not edit sudoers based only on this summary; validate syntax and account ownership on real systems.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: sudo group members followed by uncommented sudoers lines that grant privileges.

commands: awk -F: '$1=="sudo" {print "sudo group: " $4}' fixtures/user-access-audit/etc/group; grep -RhnE '^[^#].*ALL=' fixtures/user-access-audit/etc/sudoers fixtures/user-access-audit/etc/sudoers.d

shorts.title: Review sudo paths.
shorts.voiceover: Sudo access can come from groups or direct rules. Print both before approving, removing, or narrowing privilege.
shorts.caption: Privilege paths should be explicit.

linkedin.question: Do you review sudo group membership and direct sudoers rules together?
linkedin.cta: Make every privilege path visible before editing.

youtube.description: Summarize sudo group membership and sudoers grants from fixture-local stubs.

title: Count authorized_keys by User
hook: authorized_keys is the practical SSH access list.
problem: You need to find which home directories have authorized_keys files and how many active key lines each contains.
command: find fixtures/user-access-audit/home -path '*/.ssh/authorized_keys' -exec sh -c 'for f do user=$(basename "$(dirname "$(dirname "$f")")"); keys=$(grep -vc "^[[:space:]]*#" "$f"); printf "%s %s %s\n" "$user" "$keys" "$f"; done' sh {} + | sort
starting_state: Fixture-local home directories include authorized_keys files for human, deploy, emergency, and stale service accounts.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads fixture-local authorized_keys files and counts non-comment lines.
when_to_use: Use during SSH access inventory, offboarding, or server handoff when keys may outlive account ownership.
when_not_to_use: Do not remove keys just because they exist; confirm owner, source system, and current dependency first.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Usernames, active key counts, and authorized_keys file paths.

commands: find fixtures/user-access-audit/home -path '*/.ssh/authorized_keys' -exec sh -c 'for f do user=$(basename "$(dirname "$(dirname "$f")")"); keys=$(grep -vc "^[[:space:]]*#" "$f"); printf "%s %s %s\n" "$user" "$keys" "$f"; done' sh {} + | sort

shorts.title: Count SSH keys by user.
shorts.voiceover: Find authorized keys files, count active key lines, and turn scattered SSH access into a reviewable list.
shorts.caption: authorized_keys is practical access inventory.

linkedin.question: Do you count authorized_keys entries by user during offboarding?
linkedin.cta: Inventory SSH keys before removing access.

youtube.description: Count active authorized_keys lines by user from fixture-local home directories.

experiments.variant_b: Turn keys into an access list.

title: Find SSH Keys for nologin Users
hook: A nologin shell does not automatically mean SSH keys are irrelevant.
problem: You need to spot accounts that have authorized_keys files even though their passwd shell is nologin.
command: comm -12 <(awk -F: '$7 !~ /(bash|sh|zsh)$/ {print $1}' fixtures/user-access-audit/etc/passwd | sort) <(find fixtures/user-access-audit/home -path '*/.ssh/authorized_keys' -printf '%h\n' | awk -F/ '{print $(NF-1)}' | sort)
starting_state: The fixture has service-style accounts and one nologin account with an old authorized_keys file.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command compares fixture-local passwd accounts with authorized_keys owners.
when_to_use: Use when checking for stale SSH key files after service account changes or offboarding.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume every match is exploitable; SSH daemon options, forced commands, and account policy can change behavior.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Account names that are nologin in passwd but still have authorized_keys files.

commands: comm -12 <(awk -F: '$7 !~ /(bash|sh|zsh)$/ {print $1}' fixtures/user-access-audit/etc/passwd | sort) <(find fixtures/user-access-audit/home -path '*/.ssh/authorized_keys' -printf '%h\n' | awk -F/ '{print $(NF-1)}' | sort)

shorts.title: Find keys on nologin accounts.
shorts.voiceover: Compare nologin accounts against authorized keys owners to find stale SSH access files that deserve review.
shorts.caption: nologin accounts can still have key files.

linkedin.question: Do you check for authorized_keys files on service or nologin accounts?
linkedin.cta: Compare account policy with the files that grant access.

youtube.description: Compare passwd shells with authorized_keys owners to find stale SSH key files.

experiments.variant_b: Compare nologin users to SSH keys.

title: Show Successful Logins and sudo Use
hook: Access reviews need both who logged in and who elevated privileges.
problem: You need a compact access timeline from auth logs that includes successful SSH logins and sudo commands.
starting_state: A fixture-local auth log contains accepted SSH public-key logins, failed noise, and sudo command audit lines.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command filters the fixture-local auth log for successful access and privilege-use lines.
when_to_use: Use during first response, server handoff, or after a suspicious change to build a quick access timeline.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat this as a complete audit trail; also check rotated logs, system journal, and identity-provider logs on real systems.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Accepted SSH login lines and sudo command lines in log order.

shorts.title: Build an access timeline.
shorts.voiceover: Filter auth logs for accepted SSH logins and sudo lines so real access stands apart from failed background noise.
shorts.caption: Logins plus sudo make the timeline.

linkedin.question: Do you combine successful SSH logins and sudo lines when reviewing access?
linkedin.cta: Build the timeline before changing access.

youtube.description: Filter auth logs for accepted SSH public-key logins and sudo command use.

title: List Privileged Group Members
hook: Group membership can grant more access than the username suggests.
problem: You need to review accounts listed in privileged groups such as sudo, adm, or docker.
starting_state: A fixture-local group file includes administrative, log-reading, and container-runtime groups with named members.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads a fixture-local group stub and prints populated privileged groups.
when_to_use: Use during access reviews when group membership may grant root, log, or runtime control.
when_not_to_use: Do not remove group members from this output alone; confirm role ownership, automation, and approval records first.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Privileged group names followed by their listed members.

shorts.title: Review privileged groups.
shorts.voiceover: Sudo is not the only group that matters. Print privileged groups with members so hidden access paths are visible.
shorts.caption: Groups can be access paths.

linkedin.question: Do you review docker and adm membership alongside sudo during access audits?
linkedin.cta: Check privileged groups before changing users.

youtube.description: List members of privileged groups from a fixture-local group file.

experiments.variant_b: Review sudo, adm, and docker together.

title: Summarize sudo Commands by User
hook: Privilege history is easier to review when users and commands are separated.
problem: You need to extract sudo users and command paths from auth log lines.
starting_state: A fixture-local auth log contains sudo command audit lines for several accounts.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command filters sudo log lines and extracts the acting user plus command.
when_to_use: Use during access reviews, incident triage, or post-change checks when privilege use matters.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume this covers every privileged action; rotated logs, journal data, and direct root sessions may add context.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A sorted list of sudo users mapped to the commands they ran.

shorts.title: Summarize sudo history.
shorts.voiceover: Extract the sudo user and command from auth logs so privilege use becomes a short review list instead of raw log noise.
shorts.caption: Privilege history should be readable.

linkedin.question: Do you summarize sudo commands by user before reviewing the full auth log?
linkedin.cta: Turn sudo logs into a readable access timeline.

youtube.description: Extract sudo users and commands from fixture-local auth logs.

title: Review a Breakglass Account
hook: Emergency accounts should be easy to find and hard to ignore.
problem: You need to collect account, group, SSH key, and log evidence for a breakglass user from fixture-local files.
starting_state: The fixture includes a breakglass account with a login shell, sudo membership, an SSH key, and recent auth activity.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command searches fixture-local account, access, and log stubs for the breakglass username.
when_to_use: Use when auditing emergency accounts or checking whether exceptional access was used recently.
when_not_to_use: Do not delete emergency access from search results alone; verify business continuity requirements and approval policy.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Line-numbered matches showing breakglass records across account files, keys, and logs.

shorts.title: Review breakglass access.
shorts.voiceover: Emergency access should not be mysterious. Search account files, SSH keys, and logs for the breakglass user in one pass.
shorts.caption: Emergency accounts need evidence.

linkedin.question: Do you include recent log use when reviewing emergency accounts?
linkedin.cta: Collect account, key, and log evidence together.

youtube.description: Search fixture-local account files, SSH keys, and logs for a breakglass account.

title: Find SSH Key Users with sudo
hook: The highest-priority access review starts where SSH keys and sudo overlap.
problem: You need to identify users who both have authorized_keys files and appear in the sudo group.
command: comm -12 <(find fixtures/user-access-audit/home -path '*/.ssh/authorized_keys' -printf '%h\n' | awk -F/ '{print $(NF-1)}' | sort) <(awk -F: '$1=="sudo" {gsub(",","\n",$4); print $4}' fixtures/user-access-audit/etc/group | sort)
starting_state: The fixture has several authorized_keys files and a sudo group with named administrative users.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command compares fixture-local SSH key owners with sudo group members.
when_to_use: Use during access reviews to prioritize accounts that can log in by key and elevate privileges.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat this as the only privilege path; direct sudoers rules and other privileged groups can matter too.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Usernames present in both the authorized_keys owner list and the sudo group.

commands: comm -12 <(find fixtures/user-access-audit/home -path '*/.ssh/authorized_keys' -printf '%h\n' | awk -F/ '{print $(NF-1)}' | sort) <(awk -F: '$1=="sudo" {gsub(",","\n",$4); print $4}' fixtures/user-access-audit/etc/group | sort)

shorts.title: Find key users with sudo.
shorts.voiceover: Compare authorized keys owners with sudo group members. Accounts in both lists deserve the first review pass.
shorts.caption: SSH keys plus sudo is priority access.

linkedin.question: Do you prioritize accounts that have both SSH keys and sudo access?
linkedin.cta: Intersect key ownership with privileged groups.

youtube.description: Find users who both own authorized_keys files and appear in the sudo group.

experiments.variant_a: SSH keys plus sudo is priority access.
